question_text:
Three identical electromagnets (A, B, and C) are set up with different configurations:
- Electromagnet A: 12V battery, 100-turn coil, soft iron core
- Electromagnet B: 6V battery, 100-turn coil, soft iron core
- Electromagnet C: 12V battery, 50-turn coil, soft iron core

Which electromagnet will produce the strongest magnetic field, and which factors explain this?

options.label: A
options.text: Electromagnet A because it has both the highest voltage and the most turns

options.label: B
options.text: Electromagnet B because it uses the most efficient voltage for electromagnets

options.label: C
options.text: Electromagnet C because 12V is more important than the number of turns

options.label: D
options.text: All three electromagnets will be equally strong because they use the same core material

explanation: Electromagnet A is strongest because magnetic field strength depends on two key factors: voltage (which increases current through the coil) and number of turns. Electromagnet A has both advantages—12V instead of 6V and 100 turns instead of 50. Option B is incorrect because there's no 'most efficient' voltage in this context; higher voltage means more current and stronger field. Option C wrongly prioritizes voltage over turns. Option D ignores that voltage and turns both matter.
